author: @jonathancard4466
text: I had an epiphany about modulators recently (the point of the, always confused me). The modulator and the output gear operate just like a brushless motor, but the variable magnet, instead of an electromagnet, is made of a piece of metal whose field is alternated by moving permanent magnets past it. So the frequency of the change of polarity is controlled by the speed of the input shaft. One way to look at it is that the role of the motor controller is played by the input shaft; another is that the variable magnet is varying through mechanical means rather than electrical.

author: @Berkana
text: Robert, whenever I see systems like this, I wonder whether they can be enhanced by the use of Halbach arrays, since these arrays are much more efficient in the use of the available magnetic field. What are your thoughts on Halbach arrays for magnetic gears? Would they enable any designs that are not efficient or not worth doing in conventional magnetic gearing?

replies.author: @jonathancard4466
replies.text: I believe Halbach arrays are used with these a lot. They triple the number of magnets, so it gets more expensive, but they limit the amount of magnetic field interfering with surrounding components as well as increasing the amount of field usable to the gearing.

author: @simongross3122
text: I've always thought that magnets are somewhat magical.  I've seen lots of explanations, including quantum physical explanations, of how they work, but I think that all we can do is describe how they behave and we really don't know exactly how they work.  Just being able to describe behavior, even with formulae and mathematics, does not mean we understand.  It always amazes me that we are prepared to explain things we don't understand in terms of other things we don't understand.

The creative activity is more impressive than the awesome explanations - making ideas accessible is not a simple task.

Magnets do have the benefit too, of not placing significant strain on the mechanical components. The low torque density is a limitation - though low torque high speed applications exist (ie. servo drives.

The "2nd order demonstration - has applications in any "differential drive system - basically a toothless epicyclic arrangement.

author: @hunnybunnysheavymetalmusic6542
text:
Would be great, so long as the tractive forces are equal to the resistive loads.

For small, low mass applications, might be a real gem.

For large, industrial applications, might get really expensive, bulky and finicky, as well as have overheating issues.

Or it just might not have enough shear strength to move the load forward at all.

Plenty to investigate though.

I can see using this, if it works, to make machines that virtually never wear out, use very little lubricant [one very expensive and essential element of all modern machinery, and something many larger machines consume quite a lot of] , and of course, eliminate wear from direct contact, but I can also see a lot of physical heat being generated due to the massive amount of resistive magnetic flux through internal eddy currents, leading to the need for cooling, especially seeing as all permanent magnets lose their strength as the temperatures increase, which might defeat any costs savings previously gained.

At least with some coolants, it might be possible to keep the overall cost down, but each class of coolant has its own benefits and drawbacks too.

After all, there is no free ride.

author: @PlayNowWorkLater
text: Q? When you say “we don’t really understand how they work” I’m wondering who you’re referring to. Because my understanding as an Electrician, and schooling I had to do for that trade has given me a lot of insight into the history of magnets, how they are made, I.e. heating magnetic material to the point where atoms can shift and placing it in a strong magnetic field, and allowing the “newly created” magnets atoms to rearrange in such a way that they are mostly in line with each other. Then given a chance to cool, and locking the atoms orientation it becomes a magnet. Now I’m speaking as an electrician, and that’s my understanding, and I have friends who are physicists who have a deeper meaning at a quantum level. And I understand the scientific method. Theories are based on hypothesis and supported by evidence. I think it’s fair to say we, being the collective “we” as society and all its parts, including all the smarty pants out there, that we do have a pretty good idea of what is happening with magnets. Lots of evidence in technology of how they are made, how they work, etc.

replies.author: @PlayNowWorkLater
replies.text: P.s I love your content! Your enthusiasm and your understanding  of how things work brings me back again and again to your channel

replies.author: @AtlasReburdened
replies.text: He means you, your physicist friends, and everyone else. Our models of the universe and the forces and phenomena therein are merely descriptions of operating principles. Describing the effects of something is not the same as describing how it works, much less what it is. Even thorough understanding of magnetism, down to the role of virtual photons propagating the electromagnetic field through which it is expressed, is nothing but a description of operating principles, akin to a caveman knowing nothing of the atomic composition of a piece of obsidian, but knowing that it can be broken in certain ways to form a cutting edge, and thinking this to be a holistic understanding of it's nature.

author: @rodh2168
text: I owned a 1990 Subaru Justy.  First vehicle ever to have a CVT transmission.  The clutch was an electromagnet activated by a switch on the accelerator pedal.  In subsequent years the magnetic clutch was replaced by a torgue converter clutch but I liked it.  No creep when the vehicle was in gear if no throttle press.

The automatic brake sensor was is located behind the rotors and it works by magnetically sensing how fast the truck is going by a gear mounted on the rotor. I thought what a great way to use a magnet...but the worse place to put one it made the brake issues in my old rusty truck way worse lol
